With limited information, it is still understandable that this is a compact cross-run triage surface: every run has complete call coverage, but segment coverage varies and some segments are unusable against the current anchor. The report is already enough to separate clean runs from structurally degraded ones.

The obvious part is that call reviews are fully covered in the shown runs while segment reviews are not, and that the highest issue counts cluster in the same few runs. It is also obvious that "tokio-rs__tokio-5200" is a bad outlier on segment usability while "BurntSushi__ripgrep-723" is comparatively clean.

What is non-obvious is why some runs show many anchor mismatches even when persisted segment artifacts exist, and why the issue severity distribution is so skewed toward the high end. It is also not obvious whether the issue counts reflect genuine system failure or mostly repeated local search thrash from the same workflow pattern.

The underlying dataset appears to be a set of eval runs with richer protocol artifacts attached, where call-level review coverage is complete but segment-level validity depends on the segmentation version. A strong conclusion is that the bottleneck is no longer missing call data; it is the stability and reusability of segment-based analysis across anchor changes.

"Gaps" appear to mean missing usable segment-review coverage for the current segmentation anchor, not missing call reviews. "Skips" appear to mean persisted segment-review artifacts that were rejected because they no longer matched the selected anchor basis.

These commands let you learn run-level coverage, issue load, segment usability, anchor mismatch rate, and the shape of the most severe local call issues. The single-run view also gives a readable drilldown into which segments are degraded and which tool calls are carrying the strongest negative signal.

The histograms show distribution shape, not new semantics: one histogram is for segment-review usability/coverage and the other is for call issue severity. They tell you whether problems are concentrated or spread out, and whether the run is mostly mild or mostly severe.

A reasonable assumption is that the aggregate loader is working as intended and that the current report is faithful enough for triage, even if the semantic labels could be sharper. It is also reasonable to assume the segment mismatches are an artifact of re-segmentation, not random corruption.

The five highest-value missing pieces are: a breakdown of the Issues column by category, a direct count of anchor-mismatched segments, a way to compare against the previous segmentation basis, a clearer mapping from segment rows back to the exact segment-review artifact, and an intervention-ranking view across runs. Those would unlock the graph most because they would convert a descriptive table into a decision surface.

The least meaningful piece is the current label "Signal histograms," because it sounds more semantic than it is. The segment coverage histogram is especially weakly named, since it mostly reflects usable review presence rather than a true notion of understanding.

If I were designing the system, I would prioritize stabilizing the segment identity/anchor story and then add a category breakdown for the high issue counts so the next action is obvious. I would also make the all-runs view sort by a clearer intervention score rather than raw issue count alone.

The one thing I would change is the naming and decomposition of the histogram section so it reads as explicit coverage and severity distributions. The one thing I would not change is the compact all-runs table, because it immediately shows which runs are clean, which are degraded, and which deserve drilldown.

The main actionable insight is that full call coverage is already present, so the next leverage point is not more call collection but better segment stability and issue categorization. That means the report is already good enough to direct effort, even though it still needs a more discriminating summary layer.
